short.headline: Only 3 crore returns pay income tax out of nearly 8 crore filed
short.dek: The zero-tax majority is not evasion but a deliberate policy choice that keeps lifting the nil-tax ceiling. The burden rests on a tiny apex.
short.body: About 7.97 crore income-tax returns were analysed for AY2023-24, but 61.6% owed nothing. Only 3.06 crore actually paid any tax. The zero-tax share jumped sharply in AY2020-21 when the Section 87A rebate made income up to ₹5 lakh tax-free. Since then policy changes have raised the nil-tax ceiling to ₹12 lakh (effective AY2026-27), so the true zero-tax share is likely higher. The tax that is collected is intensely concentrated: about 91,000 returns (0.1% of filers) paid 58.1% of all income tax. Personal income tax now exceeds corporate tax by nearly ₹2.5 lakh crore. The system has become more progressive, with direct taxes rising to 58.8% of central revenue, and the cost to collect ₹100 of direct tax has fallen to just ₹0.41.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: Imagine a chaiwala who files his return honestly. He earns ₹4.5 lakh a year, well below the tax-free line. He files, he follows the law, but he pays zero income tax. He is part of the 6 out of 10 filers who owe nothing. The real taxpaying India is tiny — only about 3 crore people carry the entire personal income tax. And among them, the top 0.1% pay 58% of everything. The government has deliberately raised that tax-free line from ₹2 lakh to ₹12 lakh over a decade, making sure the bottom stays untaxed. So when you hear 8 crore filers, remember: that number is about paperwork, not tax. The actual burden sits on a needle point.
macha.soWhat: India's income tax is an elite tax, and that's a policy choice, not a failure.

article.title: Who actually pays income tax in India?
article.standfirst: About 8 crore returns are filed each year, but only 3 crore pay any income tax. The zero-tax majority is by design, not evasion. The burden is carried by a tiny apex, while the system has quietly become more progressive and cheaper to run.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How many people who file income tax returns actually pay anything?

Out of 79.71 million returns analysed for AY2023-24, 61.6% owed nothing. That is roughly 49.1 million filers whose tax payable was zero. Only about 30.57 million returns had any tax to pay. Filing a return is not the same as paying tax. The zero-tax share was already 55.6% in AY2012-13. It jumped sharply in AY2020-21 when the Section 87A rebate made total income up to ₹5 lakh effectively tax-free. That single policy change moved the share from around 40% to roughly two-thirds in one year. Millions of salaried workers, pensioners, and small business owners with honest incomes below ₹5 lakh suddenly owed nothing. So the majority-zero is legal exemption, not evasion. The data ends at AY2023-24, before the nil-tax ceiling was raised to ₹7 lakh (2023) and then ₹12 lakh (2025). The true zero-tax share now is almost certainly higher.

## At what income level does someone start paying income tax, and how has that changed?

The income at which tax becomes nil has been lifted again and again by Parliament. In AY2013-14, you could earn ₹2 lakh before paying any tax. By AY2020-21, the rebate pushed the ceiling to ₹5 lakh. The Interim Budget 2019 raised the rebate to ₹12,500, making incomes up to ₹5 lakh tax-free. Budget 2023 made the new regime the default and lifted the ceiling to ₹7 lakh for AY2024-25. Budget 2025 raised it again to ₹12 lakh, effective from AY2026-27. For a salaried person, the standard deduction lifts it further to about ₹12.75 lakh. Each step was a policy choice, not an accident. And each step mechanically enlarged the share of zero-tax filers. The ₹4.5 lakh to ₹5 lakh income band is the single biggest cluster of returns, with millions parked just under the cliff where tax becomes zero. Since our return-level data ends at AY2023-24, the 61.6% zero-tax share is a floor, not a ceiling.

## If most filers owe nothing, how does the taxpaying base compare to total filers?

The headline filing number is 79.71 million returns. But only 30.57 million returns actually pay any tax. The gap of over 49 million has widened over the years. In AY2012-13, 31.19 million returns were filed and 13.84 million paid tax. Filing has more than doubled but the paying base has only doubled. The true taxpayer base is about 3 crore, not 8 crore. That is roughly the number of households that carry the entire income tax. The rest are part of a widening return-filing habit, encouraged by policies like making filing mandatory for certain transactions, but they are out of the tax net by design. The gap will likely widen further as the nil-tax ceiling rises to ₹12 lakh.

## Who carries the heaviest burden of income tax?

A vanishingly small group. In AY2023-24, about 91,000 returns, 0.1% of all filers, each owed more than ₹1 crore in tax. Together they paid 58.1% of all income tax. The other roughly 3 crore returns that paid any tax split the remaining 41.9%. Returns reporting gross total income above ₹1 crore held 45.4% of all declared income. The concentration has eased slightly: in AY2012-13, the top >₹1 crore group paid 65.9% of the tax. The broadening base of small taxpayers has diluted the apex share, but it remains extreme. Income tax in India is not a broad-based levy. It rests on a needle point, and that point is getting ever sharper in absolute terms.

## Has the income-tax burden shifted from companies to individuals?

Yes, and the crossover is stark. In FY2000-01, corporate tax collections were ₹35,696 crore and personal income tax was ₹31,764 crore. For years, companies paid more. But in FY2020-21, personal tax overtook corporate tax. By FY2024-25 (provisional), personal tax reached ₹12.4 lakh crore against corporate tax of ₹9.9 lakh crore, a gap of nearly ₹2.5 lakh crore. One trigger was the steep corporate tax rate cut in September 2019, which slashed rates for existing companies to 22% and new manufacturing to 15%. That reduced corporate collections just as personal tax kept growing with formalization and higher incomes. The personal tax head includes not just individuals but HUFs, trusts, and other non-corporate entities, so it is slightly broader than pure individual tax. But the direction is clear: the tax burden has shifted decisively onto people.

## What kind of income do individual taxpayers declare: salary, business, or capital gains?

For individual taxpayers, salary is the largest source. In AY2023-24, they declared ₹35.2 lakh crore in salary income, up from ₹8.3 lakh crore in AY2013-14, a 4.2-fold rise. Business income declared by individuals was ₹16.7 lakh crore, up 3.6-fold from ₹4.7 lakh crore. But the fastest-growing stream is long-term capital gains, which jumped from ₹28,686 crore to ₹2.5 lakh crore, nearly a 9-fold increase. That surge reflects deeper equity markets, more retail investing, and some profit-booking. The individual income mix is still dominated by salary, but capital gains are climbing fast, especially at the top. These are individual-only figures; adding companies would inflate business and capital gains, but the story of the individual taxpayer is that salary still pays the bills, and capital is becoming a larger second income.

## Is the tax system becoming more progressive over time?

Direct taxes, which fall on income and profits, are generally more progressive than indirect taxes like GST that fall on spending. In FY2000-01, direct taxes made up 36.3% of central tax revenue. By FY2024-25, that share had risen to 58.8%. The only major dip was the COVID year. This 25-year tilt means the state relies more on taxing those who can pay, rather than those who must spend. But how progressive this really is depends on who within the direct-tax category bears the load. As we have seen, personal income tax is itself concentrated at the top, while corporate tax is a mixed bag. Still, the direction is towards progressivity, and that is consistent with the policy of exempting the bottom.

## How efficiently does India collect its income tax?

In FY2000-01, it cost the tax department ₹1.36 to collect ₹100 of direct tax. By FY2024-25, that cost had plummeted to ₹0.41. That is one of the cheapest collection costs in the world. The big efficiency gains came from digitisation: e-filing, pre-filled returns, and the expansion of TDS (tax deducted at source) on salaries and interest. Salaried workers have their tax deducted by employers before they see the money, which is almost costless to collect. The low cost also reflects a narrow base concentrated among formal-sector employees. A broader tax net with more self-employed and informal workers might cost more. So cheap collection is not automatically a sign of a perfect system; it is partly a sign that the department is collecting from the easiest targets.

## How does India's overall tax collection compare to other countries?

India's general government tax revenue is 17.3% of GDP, including social contributions (which are near-zero). That is mid-low. Wealthy economies like Germany (37.5%), the UK (34.9%), and even Brazil (32.7%) collect far more. China collects 22.1%, the US 24.8%, South Africa 28.1%. India is above Indonesia (10.3%) and Bangladesh (7.3%). The gap with European countries is partly structural: they have large payroll taxes to fund pensions and health, while India runs a minimal social security system. But even among Asian peers, India's tax effort is moderate. A tax-to-GDP of around 17% means the state has limited resources relative to the size of the economy, which is one reason why public spending on health and education remains low.

## How narrow is India's personal income tax base compared to the world?

Personal income tax as a share of GDP is a direct measure of how widely the income-tax net is cast. India's PIT-to-GDP is 3.7%. The OECD average is 8.2%. That gap is vast; rich countries typically raise 5-12% of GDP from individual income tax. But in Asia, India's effort is not unusually low. The Asia-Pacific average is 3.6%, almost identical. China raises only 1.1% of GDP from personal income tax, Philippines 3.1%, Singapore 2.5%, Indonesia 1.1%. India sits right on the regional norm. What is unusual is not the low effort but the extreme concentration within that 3.7%, almost three-fifths paid by 0.1% of filers. So the narrow base is a double story: low overall, and hyper-concentrated among the few who pay.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India's income tax system reveals a stark contrast: most people who file returns pay nothing, while a tiny elite shoulders nearly 60% of the total tax. This page unpacks the policy choices, shifts in burden, and international comparisons behind who really pays.

selectedDataPoints.label: Share of income-tax returns that owe zero tax, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: The central startling fact: 61.6% of filers owe nothing

selectedDataPoints.label: Income you can earn before paying any income tax, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Shows the deliberate policy escalation to ₹12 lakh

selectedDataPoints.label: Income-tax returns filed (after consistency rules), latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: The raw filing number that misleads if read alone

selectedDataPoints.label: Returns that actually pay income tax (tax payable > 0), latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Reveals the true taxpayer base of about 3 crore

selectedDataPoints.label: Who pays India's income tax: value (58.1%)
selectedDataPoints.reason: The 0.1% concentration figure for a pull quote

selectedDataPoints.label: Personal (non-corporate) income tax collected, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Shows the shift to ₹12.4 lakh crore from individuals

selectedDataPoints.label: Corporate tax collected, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Contrasts with personal tax to highlight the crossover

selectedDataPoints.label: Direct taxes as a share of central tax revenue, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Marks the rise in progressivity to 58.8%

selectedDataPoints.label: Cost of collecting ₹100 of direct tax, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: A stunning efficiency drop to just ₹0.41

selectedDataPoints.label: Tax-to-GDP ratio: India vs peers: India value 17.3%
selectedDataPoints.reason: Sets India in global context as mid-low

selectedDataPoints.label: Personal income tax as % of GDP: India vs peers: India value 3.7%
selectedDataPoints.reason: Shows the narrow personal tax base internationally

pullQuotes.quote: In AY2023-24, about 91,000 returns — 0.1% of all filers — paid 58.1% of all income tax.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Who pays India's income tax: value (58.1%)

pullQuotes.quote: The income at which tax becomes nil has moved from ₹2 lakh (2013) to ₹12 lakh (2026), each step a policy choice.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Income you can earn before paying any income tax, earliest / latest

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of it as the year the taxman checks your accounts. If you earned money in 2022–23, you pay tax in the assessment year 2023–24. All the return data here uses these AY labels, so AY2023-24 refers to income earned during FY2022-23.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Every number in the return-level analysis is tagged by assessment year, not the year the money was actually earned. Keeping this straight prevents confusion about when policy changes took effect.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is a line in the tax law that can erase your tax bill entirely if your total income is below a certain amount. It’s like a magic eraser: in AY2023-24, anyone earning up to ₹5 lakh could use it to reduce their tax to zero. The limit has since been raised.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The rebate is the single largest policy reason why the zero-tax share of filers jumped from about 40% to around 67% in one year. It is the tool Parliament uses to widen the no-tax zone.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Direct taxes are paid straight by the person or company on whom they fall, like income tax taken from your salary. Indirect taxes are collected by a shopkeeper and passed to the government, like the GST on a packet of biscuits. Direct taxes are generally fairer because they depend on how much you earn, not how much you spend.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The rising share of direct taxes — from 36% to 59% of central revenue — signals that India’s tax system is becoming more progressive, leaning on income rather than consumption.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This measures all the tax collected by central, state, and local governments as a percentage of the country’s total economic output (GDP). It includes social security contributions, which are big in Europe and tiny in India. A higher number means the government has more money to spend on roads, schools, and defence, relative to the size of the economy.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India’s 17.3% puts it in the mid-low range globally. Understanding this ratio helps see why the state’s capacity to spend is limited, and why the income tax base feels so narrow.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Just like a shop has costs to run, the Income Tax Department spends money on salaries, computers, and offices to collect tax. This cost is shown as a percentage of the total tax collected. If collecting ₹100 costs ₹0.41, that means 0.4% of the tax goes to running the collection machine.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India’s cost has fallen from ₹1.36 to ₹0.41 per ₹100 collected, one of the lowest in the world. It reflects heavy use of TDS and digitisation, but also a narrow base that is easy to reach.

glossaryBlocks.term: Personal income tax (PIT) as % of GDP
glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A narrower lens than tax-to-GDP: it looks only at the tax individuals pay on their wages, business profits, and capital gains, and compares that to the size of the economy. If this number is low, a small slice of the population is shouldering the tax, or tax rates are low.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: At 3.7% of GDP, India’s personal income tax base is less than half the rich-country average but right on the Asia-Pacific norm. The narrow base is what makes the concentration at the top so extreme.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A line chart with annual frequency shows the trend over time, which suits a phone reader for scanning the trajectory.
Policy changes are discrete steps; a line chart makes the stair-step pattern visible.
Dual lines show the growing gap clearly; annual ticks avoid clutter.
Horizontal bars make the single-year concentration comparison stark and easy on a phone.
Two lines crossing tells the story; annual data is sufficient.
A multi-line chart shows the relative growth of the three components over a decade.
A single rising line with a long history shows the secular shift.
A falling line over 24 years is a clean story.
Horizontal bars make cross-country ranking instantly legible on mobile.
Again, bars are perfect for comparing a handful of countries at a glance.

chartExplainers.title: Most filers owe nothing
chartExplainers.takeaway: 61.6% of income-tax returns analysed for AY2023-24 had zero tax payable, meaning roughly 49 million filers owed nothing.
chartExplainers.detail: The line tracks the share of e-filed returns with tax payable exactly zero. It starts at 55.6% in AY2012-13 and climbs gradually, then leaps from about 40% to 67% in AY2020-21. That jump is the direct result of the Section 87A rebate, which made incomes up to ₹5 lakh tax-free. After AY2020-21 the share stabilises around 61-64%. The data covers returns that pass CBDT consistency checks. Note that this is before the nil-tax ceiling was raised to ₹7 lakh and then ₹12 lakh in later years, so the actual zero-tax share now is almost certainly higher. The chart demolishes the lazy equation of filing with paying.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The single most surprising fact: filing a return and paying tax are very different things.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line shows the percentage of returns with zero tax payable each assessment year. The sharp jump in AY2020-21 is the 5-lakh rebate.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read the start of the series as 'half didn't pay'; the level was already high, then policy pushed it higher.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a clean line chart with large labels on the y-axis and sparse year ticks on the x-axis.

chartExplainers.title: The income you can earn before paying any tax
chartExplainers.takeaway: The nil-tax ceiling has been raised from ₹2 lakh (AY2013-14) to ₹12 lakh (AY2026-27) through a series of policy decisions.
chartExplainers.detail: This line stairs upward as successive Finance Acts extend the reach of the Section 87A rebate and adjust the basic exemption. The five key steps visible are: ₹2 lakh (up to AY2013-14), a gradual rise via rebate tweaks, the landmark ₹5 lakh from AY2020-21, ₹7 lakh from AY2024-25 after the new regime became default, and ₹12 lakh from AY2026-27. Each horizontal segment is a stable policy period; each vertical jump is a budget announcement. The final ₹12 lakh point is outside the current ITR data window, but it signals that the zero-tax share will climb further. For salaried persons, the standard deduction adds about ₹75,000 more, taking the practical ceiling to approximately ₹12.75 lakh.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The zero-tax majority is engineered by policy: Parliament keeps lifting the income at which tax becomes nil.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line tracks the total income (₹ lakh) below which an individual pays zero income tax, including rebate effects. The y-axis shows ₹ lakh.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is the general individual ceiling; actual take-home for salaried persons can be higher due to standard deduction.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a line chart with step markers at each policy change year; label the ₹5 lakh and ₹12 lakh thresholds clearly.

chartExplainers.title: Returns filed vs returns that pay
chartExplainers.takeaway: Total returns filed have risen to 79.71 million, but only 30.57 million actually pay any income tax.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines: the upper line is all returns analysed (after consistency checks), and the lower line is returns with positive tax payable. The wedge between them — the zero-tax majority — widens over time. In AY2012-13, 31.19 million were filed and 13.84 million paid. By AY2023-24, filing more than doubled while paying roughly doubled, so the gap ballooned from about 17 million to 49 million. The gap is not a sign of evasion; it mirrors the rising nil-tax ceiling and the expansion of return-filing requirements for non-tax reasons (like high-value transactions). The true taxpayer base remains about 3 crore.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The true taxpaying base is about 3 crore, not the headline ~8 crore filers.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines: total returns filed (upper) and returns with positive tax payable (lower). The growing gap is the zero-tax majority.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume the lower line is stagnating; it has grown, but the upper line has grown even faster.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a dual-line chart with distinct colours and labels; annotate the latest gap value.

chartExplainers.title: 0.1% of filers pay most of the income tax
chartExplainers.takeaway: About 91,000 returns (0.1% of filers) that each owe more than ₹1 crore in tax pay 58.1% of all income tax.
chartExplainers.detail: Two horizontal segments sum to 100% of tax paid. The first segment, 58.1%, is labelled “~91,000 returns (top 0.1% of filers) each owing over ₹1 crore”. The rest is “every other taxpayer combined”, 41.9%. The extreme concentration means that if you lined up all taxpayers, the top sliver less than one-pixel wide would carry nearly three-fifths of the burden. This concentration has eased slightly — in AY2012-13 the top group paid 65.9% — but remains overwhelming. The tiny number of filers in the apex also explains why the cost of collection is so low: a handful of very large payers requires little administrative effort.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Income tax in India rests on a needle point: a tiny group at the top pays most of it.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two segments: the tiny sliver representing about 91,000 returns that pay over ₹1 crore each in tax vs everyone else. The chart uses percentage shares, not absolute numbers of filers.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is shares of total tax paid, not the number of filers. The top segment is less than one pixel wide if drawn to scale.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a vertical share strip or horizontal bar with a clear label on the small segment.

chartExplainers.title: Individuals now out-pay companies
chartExplainers.takeaway: Personal (non-corporate) income tax collections have overtaken corporate tax since FY2020-21, reaching ₹12.4 lakh crore in FY2024-25 versus ₹9.9 lakh crore for corporate tax.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines start near each other in FY2000-01: ₹31,764 crore personal and ₹35,696 crore corporate. They rise together, with corporate generally ahead, until FY2020-21 when personal overtakes. The gap then widens sharply, exceeding ₹2.48 lakh crore by FY2024-25 provisional. The inflection point coincides with the September 2019 corporate tax rate cuts, which reduced the effective rate for many companies. Meanwhile, personal tax collections were buoyed by formalisation, growing salaries, and compliance measures. The personal tax line includes non-corporate entities like HUFs and trusts, so it is a hairsbreadth wider than pure individual tax.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The quiet structural shift: the personal income tax has overtaken corporate tax.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines showing annual collections in ₹ crore (use labels like ₹ lakh crore on the y-axis). The crossover happens around FY2020-21.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The label 'individuals' is shorthand; the actual tax head includes non-corporate entities besides individuals.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a dual-line chart with a clear annotation at the crossover point.

chartExplainers.title: How individuals' income is earned
chartExplainers.takeaway: Salary is the largest source of declared income for individual taxpayers at ₹35.2 lakh crore, but long-term capital gains grew nine-fold over the decade to ₹2.5 lakh crore.
chartExplainers.detail: Three lines trace income declared by individual taxpayers only: salary, business, and long-term capital gains (LTCG). Salary starts at ₹8.3 lakh crore in AY2013-14 and climbs steadily to ₹35.2 lakh crore in AY2023-24. Business income rises from ₹4.7 lakh crore to ₹16.7 lakh crore. LTCG begins at just ₹28,686 crore but shoots up to ₹2.5 lakh crore, a nine-fold multiplication, driven by stock market participation and asset price growth. The takeaway: even though salary still dwarfs other sources, the income mix at the top is increasingly tilted toward financial assets — a wealth effect that taxes partly capture.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: For individual taxpayers, where does the declared income actually come from?
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three lines for salary, business, and long-term capital gains on a ₹ crore scale. Salary is the thickest line; LTCG is the steepest riser.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are individuals only; all-taxpayer totals would inflate business and capital gains with company income.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A multi-line chart with legend and sparse year ticks works well on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: The system tilted toward direct taxes
chartExplainers.takeaway: Direct taxes now make up 58.8% of central tax revenue, up from 36.3% in FY2000-01, marking a shift towards greater progressivity.
chartExplainers.detail: A single rising line traces the share of direct taxes in the Centre's total tax kitty. It starts at 36.3% in FY2000-01, crosses 50% around FY2007-08, and reaches 58.8% in FY2024-25. The only interruption is a dip in FY2019-20 and FY2020-21, when the corporate tax cut and the pandemic temporarily reduced corporate collections. The long upward climb means the tax system relies less on regressive indirect taxes (GST, excise) and more on income and profits — a structure that, in theory, taxes the better-off at a higher rate. The caveat is that progressivity depends on the internal distribution of direct taxes, which we know is highly concentrated.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Direct taxes (on income) are more progressive than indirect taxes (on spending).
chartExplainers.howToRead: A line showing the percentage of central tax revenue that comes from direct taxes, rising from about one-third to nearly three-fifths.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: A high direct-tax share is a proxy for progressivity but not a guarantee; the composition of direct taxes matters.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line chart with a clear y-axis label and a few annotation points.

chartExplainers.title: Among the cheapest tax systems to run
chartExplainers.takeaway: The cost of collecting ₹100 of direct tax has fallen from ₹1.36 in FY2000-01 to ₹0.41 in FY2024-25, making it one of the cheapest collection systems globally.
chartExplainers.detail: The line slopes down steadily from 1.4% to 0.4% over 24 years. Early dips are gradual; the decline accelerates after 2010 as e-filing and TDS coverage expand. TDS on salaries, interest, and dividends is collected by the payer and remitted directly, cutting administrative cost to near zero. The system’s cheapness is not only about efficiency; it also reflects the fact that the department mostly harvests from a narrow band of formal-sector employees and large corporates. If the tax net genuinely widened into the informal self-employed, collection costs would likely rise. Still, a cost ratio below half a percent is remarkable by any global standard.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: How much it costs the state to collect ₹100 of direct tax.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A falling line: cost per ₹100 collected has dropped from ₹1.36 to ₹0.41. The y-axis is in % of collection, starting at 1.5.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Low cost does not automatically mean efficient; it can reflect a base that is easy to reach (salaried workers with TDS).
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line chart with a clear downward trend; annotate the start and end values.

chartExplainers.title: India's tax-to-GDP is mid-low
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's general government tax revenue is 17.3% of GDP, above Indonesia and Bangladesh but below China, South Africa, Brazil, the US, the UK, and Germany.
chartExplainers.detail: Nine horizontal bars rank countries by total tax as a percentage of GDP. Germany tops at 37.5%, followed by the UK (34.9%), Brazil (32.7%), South Africa (28.1%), the US (24.8%), China (22.1%), India (17.3%), Indonesia (10.3%), and Bangladesh (7.3%). The figures include social security contributions, which are substantial in Europe and negligible in India. So part of the gap with Germany or the UK is structural — their tax systems are also pension and health insurance collectors. Compared to Asian peers, India’s effort is moderate. The mid-low rank explains why the state’s spending on public goods feels constrained.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: How much of the economy the state collects in tax, versus peers.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars showing tax revenue as a percentage of GDP for nine countries, sorted from highest to lowest. India’s bar at 17.3% sits between China and Indonesia.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These figures include social contributions, which are large in Europe and near-zero in India, so the gap is partly structural.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with country names and percentages inside or next to the bars.

chartExplainers.title: Personal income tax: a narrow base
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's personal income tax is 3.7% of GDP, less than half the OECD average of 8.2% but exactly on the Asia-Pacific average of 3.6%.
chartExplainers.detail: Eight bars rank jurisdictions by personal income tax as a share of GDP. The OECD average leads at 8.2%, then Korea at 5.7%. The Asia-Pacific average is 3.6%. India comes next at 3.7%, almost identical to the regional average. Philippines follows at 3.1%, Singapore at 2.5%, China and Indonesia both at 1.1%. The chart confirms that India’s base is narrow, but not uniquely so in its neighbourhood. The narrowness, however, combines with extreme concentration: India raises a moderate amount from personal tax, but nearly 60% of it comes from 0.1% of filers. So the problem is not just the size of the pie but how it is sliced.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The sharpest cross-country cut: how much personal income tax each economy raises.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars showing personal income tax as % of GDP. The OECD average bar is longest; India’s bar is in the middle range, longer than China’s.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: India's non-corporate tax is slightly broader than the OECD's 'individuals only', so the comparison is approximate but directionally correct.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use horizontal bars with clear labels; highlight the India bar with a distinct colour.

sectionVisualMap.heading: How many people who file income tax returns actually pay anything?
At what income level does someone start paying income tax, and how has that changed?
If most filers owe nothing, how does the taxpaying base compare to total filers?
Who carries the heaviest burden of income tax?
Has the income-tax burden shifted from companies to individuals?
What kind of income do individual taxpayers declare: salary, business, or capital gains?
Is the tax system becoming more progressive over time?
How efficiently does India collect its income tax?
How does India's overall tax collection compare to other countries?
How narrow is India's personal income tax base compared to the world?

sourceNotes: Data from CBDT Income Tax Return Statistics, Time-Series publications, and Finance Acts (for nil-tax ceiling).
International comparisons from UNU-WIDER/ICTD Government Revenue Dataset (tax-to-GDP) and OECD Revenue Statistics in Asia-Pacific; India’s PIT-to-GDP from CBDT.

All ITR figures are for assessment year (AY), which taxes income earned in the previous financial year. Return-level data covers returns that pass CBDT consistency checks, not every single return filed.
caveats: The ITR data window ends at AY2023-24, when the nil-tax ceiling was ₹5 lakh. Policy changes since then (₹7 lakh from AY2024-25, ₹12 lakh from AY2026-27) mean the current zero-tax share of filers is almost certainly higher than the 61.6% reported.

The 'personal (non-corporate) income tax' head includes individuals, HUFs, trusts, and other non-corporate entities, making it slightly broader than the OECD's 'individuals only' category used in cross-country comparisons.
Tax-to-GDP figures include social security contributions, which are large in European countries and near-zero in India, so the gap partly reflects structural differences in the welfare state.
The cost of collection is for direct taxes only; it benefits from a heavy reliance on TDS from formal-sector salaries and corporate payments.
All return counts are post-consistency checks by CBDT; actual filings may be marginally higher.
lockedNumbersUsed: Share of income-tax returns that owe zero tax, latest
Share of income-tax returns that owe zero tax, earliest
Income-tax returns filed (after consistency rules), latest
Income-tax returns filed (after consistency rules), earliest
Income you can earn before paying any income tax, latest
Income you can earn before paying any income tax, earliest
Returns that actually pay income tax (tax payable > 0), latest
Returns that actually pay income tax (tax payable > 0), earliest
Share of all declared income held by returns above ₹1 crore, latest
Share of all income tax paid by returns owing above ₹1 crore, latest
Who pays India's income tax: value (58.1% and 41.9%)
Personal (non-corporate) income tax collected, latest
Corporate tax collected, latest
Direct taxes as a share of central tax revenue, latest
Cost of collecting ₹100 of direct tax, latest
Salary income (individuals) declared in returns, latest
Business income (individuals) declared in returns, latest
Long-term capital gains (individuals) declared in returns, latest
Tax-to-GDP ratio: India vs peers: India value 17.3%
Tax-to-GDP ratio: India vs peers: Germany 37.5%, UK 34.9%, Brazil 32.7%, South Africa 28.1%, US 24.8%, China 22.1%, Indonesia 10.3%, Bangladesh 7.3%
Personal income tax as % of GDP: India vs peers: India 3.7%, OECD avg 8.2%, Korea 5.7%, Asia-Pacific avg 3.6%, Philippines 3.1%, Singapore 2.5%, China 1.1%, Indonesia 1.1%

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: Only about 3 crore returns pay income tax out of nearly 8 crore filed, because deliberate policy exemptions have made the zero-tax majority the norm; the tax that is collected comes overwhelmingly from a tiny elite, while the system has become more progressive and cheaper to run.
requiredConcepts: Filing and paying are different: 7.97 crore returns analysed, 4.91 crore (61.6%) zero tax payable, only about 3.06 crore returns actually pay.
Zero-tax majority is statutory exemption, not evasion: zero-tax share jumped from ~40% to ~67% when Section 87A made income up to ₹5 lakh tax-free.
Nil-tax ceiling raised repeatedly by policy: from ₹2 lakh (2013) to ₹12 lakh (2026).
Concentration: about 91,000 returns (0.1% of filers) pay 58% of all income tax.
Personal income tax now exceeds corporate tax by over ₹2.5 lakh crore (FY2024-25 provisional).
For individuals, salary is the largest income source; long-term capital gains grew the fastest (~9-fold over a decade).
Tax-to-GDP is mid-low at about 17% of GDP; personal income tax is narrow at 3.7% of GDP.
Direct taxes rose from 36% to 59% of central revenue, making the system more progressive.
Cost of collecting ₹100 of direct tax fell from ₹1.36 to ₹0.41.

sectionPlan.heading: How many people who file income tax returns actually pay anything?
sectionPlan.job: Explain that most returns owe zero tax, the zero-tax share has risen, and the key threshold jump.
lockedNumbersToUse: Share of income-tax returns that owe zero tax, latest
Share of income-tax returns that owe zero tax, earliest
conceptsToExplain: Zero tax payable does not mean evasion; it means legal exemption.
Assessment year (AY) vs financial year.
Return-analysis numbers are post-consistency checks.
sectionPlan.caveat: Data ends at AY2023-24, when the nil-tax ceiling was ₹5 lakh; with recent policy changes the zero-tax share is almost certainly higher now.

sectionPlan.heading: At what income level does someone start paying income tax, and how has that changed?
sectionPlan.job: Show the nil-tax ceiling history and link each policy step to the jump in the zero-tax share.
lockedNumbersToUse: Income you can earn before paying any income tax, latest
Income you can earn before paying any income tax, earliest
conceptsToExplain: Basic exemption limit vs effective nil-tax ceiling through rebates.
Section 87A rebate mechanics.
The 4.5–5 lakh income band bunching.
sectionPlan.caveat: The ₹12 lakh ceiling is effective only from AY2026-27, after our data window; for salaried persons, standard deduction raises it further to ~₹12.75 lakh.

sectionPlan.heading: If most filers owe nothing, how does the taxpaying base compare to total filers?
sectionPlan.job: Contrast the headline filing number with the much smaller number of returns that actually pay any tax.
lockedNumbersToUse: Income-tax returns filed (after consistency rules), latest
Returns that actually pay income tax (tax payable > 0), latest
conceptsToExplain: Returns filed vs returns paying: a gap of over 4.9 crore.
The true taxpayer base is about 3 crore, not 8 crore.
sectionPlan.caveat: The gap has grown as filing widened but the nil-tax ceiling rose; it will likely widen further.

sectionPlan.heading: Who carries the heaviest burden of income tax?
sectionPlan.job: Reveal the extreme concentration: a tiny fraction of filers pays most of the tax.
lockedNumbersToUse: Who pays India's income tax: value (58.1% and 41.9%)
Share of all income tax paid by returns owing above ₹1 crore, latest
Share of all declared income held by returns above ₹1 crore, latest
conceptsToExplain: Concentration ratio among taxpayers.
The ₹1 crore tax-payable threshold represents about 0.1% of filers.
sectionPlan.caveat: Concentration has slightly declined from earlier years (65.9% in 2012), partly because the base of small payers has broadened.

sectionPlan.heading: Has the income-tax burden shifted from companies to individuals?
sectionPlan.job: Show the crossover where personal income tax collections overtook corporate tax and the current gap.
lockedNumbersToUse: Personal (non-corporate) income tax collected, latest
Corporate tax collected, latest
conceptsToExplain: Personal (non-corporate) tax includes individuals, HUFs, trusts, etc., broader than pure individual tax.
Corporate tax rate cuts (2019) partially explain the crossover.
sectionPlan.caveat: The gap is over ₹2.5 lakh crore as of FY2024-25 provisional; the trend reflects both policy and the growing formal economy.

sectionPlan.heading: What kind of income do individual taxpayers declare: salary, business, or capital gains?
sectionPlan.job: Show the income composition for individuals and highlight the rapid rise of capital gains.
lockedNumbersToUse: Salary income (individuals) declared in returns, latest
Business income (individuals) declared in returns, latest
Long-term capital gains (individuals) declared in returns, latest
conceptsToExplain: Salary remains the largest single source for individuals.
Long-term capital gains grew 9-fold in a decade, a sign of financialisation.
These are individuals only, not company-inflated totals.
sectionPlan.caveat: Business income for individuals is still large (₹16.7 lakh crore), but salary leads at ₹35.2 lakh crore.

sectionPlan.heading: Is the tax system becoming more progressive over time?
sectionPlan.job: Show the rise in the direct-tax share of central revenue, which implies more reliance on income-based taxes.
lockedNumbersToUse: Direct taxes as a share of central tax revenue, latest
Direct taxes as a share of central tax revenue, earliest
conceptsToExplain: Direct taxes (on income/profits) are more progressive than indirect taxes (on spending).
The share rose from 36% to 59% over 25 years.
sectionPlan.caveat: A high direct-tax share is not automatically equitable; it depends on who bears the tax within that category.

sectionPlan.heading: How efficiently does India collect its income tax?
sectionPlan.job: Show the plummeting cost of collection, making the tax system one of the cheapest to run globally.
lockedNumbersToUse: Cost of collecting ₹100 of direct tax, latest
Cost of collecting ₹100 of direct tax, earliest
conceptsToExplain: Cost of collection includes administrative costs as a percentage of tax collected.
A 70% drop in cost over 24 years.
sectionPlan.caveat: Low cost does not automatically mean high effectiveness; it could also reflect a narrow base that is easy to collect from.

sectionPlan.heading: How does India's overall tax collection compare to other countries?
sectionPlan.job: Place India's general government tax-to-GDP ratio in international context, showing it is mid-low.
lockedNumbersToUse: Tax-to-GDP ratio: India vs peers: all values
conceptsToExplain: Tax-to-GDP measures the total tax take as a share of the economy.
Includes all government levels and social contributions.
sectionPlan.caveat: Social contributions (large in Europe) are near-zero in India, so the gap is partly structural.

sectionPlan.heading: How narrow is India's personal income tax base compared to the world?
sectionPlan.job: Show personal income tax as a percentage of GDP for India and peers, highlighting the narrow base.
lockedNumbersToUse: Personal income tax as % of GDP: India vs peers: all values
conceptsToExplain: Personal income tax (PIT) to GDP is the broadest measure of the income-tax base.
India's PIT-to-GDP is about 3.7%, less than half the OECD average.
sectionPlan.caveat: India's figure is above the Asia-Pacific average and far above China, so the base is narrow but not uniquely so in the region.

chartPlan.title: Most filers owe nothing
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The single most surprising fact: filing a return and paying tax are very different things.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line shows the percentage of returns with zero tax payable each year. The sharp jump in 2020 is the 5-lakh rebate.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read the start of the series as 'half didn't pay'; the level was already high, then policy pushed it higher.

chartPlan.title: The income you can earn before paying any tax
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The zero-tax majority is engineered by policy: Parliament keeps lifting the income at which tax becomes nil.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line tracks the total income (₹ lakh) below which an individual pays zero income tax, including rebate effects.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is the general individual ceiling; actual take-home for salaried persons can be higher due to standard deduction.

chartPlan.title: Returns filed vs returns that pay
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The true taxpaying base is about 3 crore, not the headline ~8 crore filers.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines: total returns filed (upper) and returns with positive tax payable (lower). The gap is the zero-tax majority.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume the lower line is stagnating; it has grown, but the upper line has grown even faster.

chartPlan.title: 0.1% of filers pay most of the income tax
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Income tax in India rests on a needle point: a tiny group at the top pays most of it.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two segments: the tiny sliver representing about 91,000 returns that pay over ₹1 crore each in tax vs everyone else.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is shares of total tax paid, not the number of filers. The top segment is less than one pixel wide if drawn to scale.

chartPlan.title: Individuals now out-pay companies
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The quiet structural shift: the personal income tax has overtaken corporate tax.
chartPlan.howToRead: Two lines showing annual collections in ₹ crore. The crossover happened around FY2020-21.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: NPers. tax includes non-corporate entities besides individuals; the title uses 'individuals' as shorthand.

chartPlan.title: How individuals' income is earned
chartPlan.whyShowThis: For individual taxpayers, where does the declared income actually come from?
chartPlan.howToRead: Three lines for salary, business, and long-term capital gains. Salary is the thickest; LTCG is the fastest-rising.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are individuals only; all-taxpayer totals would inflate business and capital gains with company income.

chartPlan.title: The system tilted toward direct taxes
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Direct taxes (on income) are more progressive than indirect taxes (on spending).
chartPlan.howToRead: A line showing the percentage of central tax revenue that comes from direct taxes, rising from about one-third to nearly three-fifths.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A high direct-tax share is a proxy for progressivity but not a guarantee; the composition of direct taxes matters.

chartPlan.title: Among the cheapest tax systems to run
chartPlan.whyShowThis: How much it costs the state to collect ₹100 of direct tax.
chartPlan.howToRead: A falling line: cost per ₹100 collected has dropped from ₹1.36 to ₹0.41 over 24 years.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Low cost does not automatically mean efficient; it can reflect a base that is easy to reach (salaried workers with TDS).

chartPlan.title: India's tax-to-GDP is mid-low
chartPlan.whyShowThis: How much of the economy the state collects in tax, versus peers.
chartPlan.howToRead: Horizontal bars showing tax revenue as a percentage of GDP for nine countries. India at 17.3% sits between China and Indonesia.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These figures include social contributions, which are large in Europe and near-zero in India, so the gap is partly structural.

chartPlan.title: Personal income tax: a narrow base
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The sharpest cross-country cut: how much personal income tax each economy raises.
chartPlan.howToRead: Horizontal bars showing personal income tax as % of GDP. OECD average is 8.2%; India is 3.7%, above China's 1.1%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: India's non-corporate tax is slightly broader than the OECD's 'individuals only', so the comparison is approximate.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A line chart with annual frequency shows the trend over time, which suits a phone reader for scanning the trajectory.
Policy changes are discrete steps; a line chart makes the stair-step pattern visible.
Dual lines show the growing gap clearly; annual ticks avoid clutter.
Horizontal bars make the single-year concentration comparison stark and easy on a phone.
Two lines crossing tells the story; annual data is sufficient.
A multi-line chart shows the relative growth of the three components over a decade.
A single rising line with a long history shows the secular shift.
A falling line over 24 years is a clean story.
Horizontal bars make cross-country ranking instantly legible on mobile.
Again, bars are perfect for comparing a handful of countries at a glance.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The year in which your income is assessed and you pay tax. For example, AY2023-24 taxes the income you earned in the financial year 2022-23. All ITR data is by assessment year.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Every number in the return-level data is tagged by AY, not the year the money was earned.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A tax rebate that reduces your tax to zero if your total income is below a certain threshold. It is the main policy tool used to raise the nil-tax ceiling. In AY2023-24, income up to ₹5 lakh was effectively tax-free because of this rebate.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The rebate is the single biggest reason why the zero-tax share jumped from ~40% to ~67% in one year.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Direct taxes are paid directly by the person or company on whom they are levied (like income tax). Indirect taxes are collected by an intermediary and passed on (like GST on a shampoo bottle). Direct taxes are generally considered more progressive because they fall on income, not spending.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The rising share of direct taxes is a marker of progressivity in the overall tax system.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Total tax collected by the government (central, state, and local) as a percentage of the country's GDP. It is the broadest measure of how much of the economy the state takes in tax. Social security contributions are included for international comparability.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It sets India's overall tax effort in global context. India is mid-low, partly because its social security system is minimal.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The amount the tax department spends on administration (salaries, offices, IT) to collect tax, expressed as a percentage of the total tax collected. A falling cost of collection suggests the system is becoming more efficient.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's cost of collecting ₹100 of direct tax plummeted from ₹1.36 to ₹0.41 in 24 years, one of the cheapest in the world.

glossaryPlan.term: Personal income tax (PIT) as % of GDP
glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A narrower measure than tax-to-GDP: it looks only at the tax individuals pay on their income (wages, business profits, capital gains) as a share of GDP. It captures how widely the income-tax net is cast.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's PIT-to-GDP is 3.7%, less than half the OECD average, illustrating the narrow base that underpins the concentration story.

proseRules: No AI tells (delve, tapestry, complex interplay, moreover, furthermore, in conclusion).
Every number in prose must come from a lockedNumber in the evidence packet.
No outside facts, no invented benchmarks, no comparisons not in the data.
Question-led H2 headings that answer the reader's real curiosity.
Round numbers like a human editor: 7.97 crore, not 79,712,145.
Use the displayValue from lockedNumbers when available.
One idea per sentence; short, concrete Indian English.
Avoid hedged mush: be direct but fair, and never claim causation without evidence.

evidence.question: Who actually pays income tax in India?

theme.label: Economy and public money
readerQuestions: What is the headline economic number?
Is this a total number or a per-person number?
How much of the story is prices, exchange rates, or GDP share?
What does this explain about household life?

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
